status monitoring report of the day care children  beneficiaries  after
    the implementation of the Supplementary Feeding Program under BUB Fund,
as provided for in the approved Project Proposal, and at the same  time
    to determine the attainment of the objectives of the program.

We recommended that the City Mayor:


      a) Require the GAD Focal Person to prepare on time the annual  GAD
         Plan and Budget (GPB) for  review  and  approval  by  DILG  and
         submit the approved copy and the  corresponding  Accomplishment
         Report to the COA  Audit  Team  within  the  prescribed  period
         provided in COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014; and


      b) Approve charges and disbursements on GAD Fund only if supported
         with DILG reviewed and approved GPB to ascertain  validity  and
         propriety of disbursements.

We recommended that the City Accountant  reclassify  the  procured
Truck Mounted Sewer Jetting and Vacuum Combination Equipment charged to
Other Machinery and Equipment account amounting  to  ₱20,980,000.00  to
Heavy and  Disaster  Equipment  account  to  reflect  correct  accounts
balances.

We   recommended   that   the   LSB    refrain    from    charging
salaries/allowances of locally-hired Child Development Teachers  and/or
Day Care Workers, etc. under the ECCD Program, to ensure the  legality
of the disbursements.


We also recommended that the City Mayor, City Budget Officer, City
Accountant and the City Schools Division  Superintendent  refrain  from
approving, obligating, processing and paying   subsequent  obligations
and disbursements without specific appropriation in the  Local  School
Board (LSB) Budget.

We recommended that the City Accountant certify and the City Mayor
approve any subsequent  payments  to  the  Environmental  and  Sanitary
Services only if supported with documents pertaining to  remittance  by
the  contractor/payee  to  BIR,   SSS   and   Pag-Ibig   for   complete
documentation  and  to  ascertain  the  validity   and   propriety   of
disbursement.


We recommended also that the Head of Department of Public Services
require the submission by the  contractor/payee  of  documents  on  the
monthly remittance to BIR, SSS and  Pag-Ibig  in  compliance  with  the
terms and conditions of the two  (2)  Contracts  of  Services  and  for
attachment to the disbursement voucher.

We recommended that the City Development Council ensure that  only
projects that partake the nature of investment or  capital  expenditure
are included in the 20%  Annual  Development  Plan  to  facilitate  the
immediate implementation of the projects.


We also recommended that the City Planning and Development Office,
the City Engineer’s Office and other offices that are involved  in  the
implementation of 20% Development Fund  projects,  immediately  address
the  causes  of  delays  and  fast  track  the  implementation  of  the
unimplemented projects. Regular monitoring  of  project  implementation
should be assigned to specific office to ensure implementation and  the
achievement of the desirable  outcomes  from  the  delivery  of  socio-
economic development and environmental services to the constituents.

We recommended that the  Local  School  Board  (LSB)  maximize  the
utilization of the Special Education Fund (SEF) for the  intended  PPAs
to ensure the attainment of the purposes.

We recommended that the City Mayor thru the Head  of  Department
of Public Services, submit program of works specifying the  detailed
estimates of the contract of services on Management and Operation of
Landfill Facility.


We  recommended  that  the  City  Mayor  thru  the  DPS   submit
accomplishment report duly verified by the project in-charge  as  to
the actual accomplishment based on the  approved  program  of  works.
Further, payments on the bill  of  quantities  should be based on the
actual accomplishment with necessary supporting documents.

Recommendation:
We have recommended the following:
a.)	The Local Chief Executive should create an Inventory Committee to conduct complete physical inventory count of all PPE and submit Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE).
b) Instruct the General Services Officer/Supply Officer and the Acting Municipal Accountant to regularly reconcile their records and review the composition of the PPE account to come up with an accurate Property, Plant and Equipment account balance.

Recommendation:
We recommended to direct the General Services Office to update the list of properties that needs to be insured and submit the required documents to GSIS. This will ensure that all government properties are adequately covered by insurance in order to protect the interest of the government.
Likewise, require the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate budget for the processing and payment of premiums to ensure that government properties are fully covered with insurance.

Recommendations:
We recommended to Local Chief Executive to:
a.) Direct the Municipal Treasurer to submit disbursement vouchers after payment with complete supporting documents to the Office of Municipal Accountant.
b.) Direct the Acting Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere to the prescribed time frame for the submission of monthly and quarterly reports as well as transmitting all receipts and disbursement records with complete supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor.
c.) Direct the Acting Municipal Accountant to closely coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer for the latter to submit immediately the required reports within the prescribed period, and to facilitate the recording of the transactions in the books of accounts.

Recommendations:
We recommended the following:
a.) 	Require the Human Resource Management Officer to compute the accumulated leave credits of LGU employees.
b.) 	Require the Municipal Accountant to record the Terminal Leave Benefits due to 	the employees to ensure proper recording and monitoring of earned leave benefits, in the absence of budget, disclosed it in the Notes to Financial Statements.
c.) 	Direct the Municipal Budget Officer and Human Resource Management Officer to institute prioritization of payments to the employees soon to retire.

Recommendations:
We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to:
Direct the head of Human Resource Management Office to submit the contract of service of the job orders and consultants with specific duties and functions and reconcile the manpower complement based on the actual manpower.
Consider either one of the following:
Appoint Job Order personnel as regular employees if they are qualified and believes that their services are essential and contributory to the improvement of agency’s operations or,
Stop hiring Job Order and fill up the vacant plantilla positions in accordance with the Civil Service Commission rules and regulations. Prioritize the division with undermanned in the filling-up of vacant plantilla position.
c.) 	Formulate a policy and internal control in the hiring of job order workers such as but not limited to requiring specific and detailed description of duties and     responsibilities to eliminate workings having no definite purpose, if any.

We recommended that the GSO update records on acquisitions and disposals in the PPE cards for Machinery and Equipment, Furniture and Fixtures, Transportation Equipment and other PPEs for reconciliation with APIR, AREs and with subsidiary ledgers maintained at the City Accountant’s Office. Regular reconciliation shall be conducted by the GSO to ascertain the correctness of the PPE accounts balances and for financial statements presentation.
We further recommended that the GSO maintain property cards on Land, Land Improvements, Buildings and Other Structures for reconciliation with balances shown in the Subsidiary Ledgers maintained at the City Accountant’s Office.
We recommended that the Physical Inventory Committee submit list of PPEs not found during the conduct of physical count to determine losses and liability of persons accountable as provided in Section 149 of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 23, 1992.
We recommended that the City Accountant to meanwhile, reclassify to Other Assets account and the GSO to dispose the 1,067 unserviceable PPE items amounting to ₱18,763,431.10 to reflect the fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: CMO endorsed to COA on 02/21/19 the annual physical inventory report as of December 31, 2018 of all PPEs submitted by GSO.
observations.reason: Other PPE accounts such as Land, Land Improvemen ts, Buildings and Other Structures, and Books were not included in the Annual Physical Inventory Report. Property cards were not updated and not reconciled with PPE records in the Office of the City Accountant.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Engineer’s Office regularly prepare and submit Status Report on the Accomplishment of Projects as basis by the City Accountant’s Office in transferring/reclassi fying CIP accounts to appropriate PPE and Public Infrastructure accounts for completed projects.
We further recommended that the City Accountant and the City Engineer’s Office exert efforts in the reconciliation of their records regarding completed and on-going projects.
We recommended also that the City Mayor and the Acting City Engineer immediately address the problems encountered on unimplemented projects and speed up the implementation of both partially and unimplemented projects to avoid losses and to attain the intended purposes.
observations.managementAction: Reconcili a tion of accounts is continuous and adjustments are being made on CIP projects which need s adjustment s and transferre d to Public Infrastructu res in the books.
observations.reason: The City Accounting Office relies on the completed records of disburseme nts and payrolls for the CIP. There are expenses pertaining to the completed projects which are not being paid upon completion. Though the construc ti on of the projects is physically completed when the expenses and other costs are not yet paid as there are suppliers who have not yet processed their documents, the COA has to wait for payment to record Public Infrastructu re.

observations.managementAction: The Office of the City Accountant sent demand/rem inder letters to concerned officers and employees to liquidate the r unliquidate d accounts .
observations.reason: Other accounts were not sent with demand letter due to the unknown addresses.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor, through the City Legal Office and City Accounting Office, require the NGOs/POs to submit liquidation report for financial assistance whose programs, projects or activities were already completed. Moreover, the City Mayor should designate a focal person/office to take charge of the regular monitoring and inspection on the accomplishment of the intended purpose. The City Accountant, before the release of financial assistance, should certify that the previous cash advance granted to the NGO/PO has been liquidated, liquidation documents are post-audited and properly taken up in the books to ascertain the validity of liquidation.
We recommended that the City Accountant make correction on the erroneous entries made on the liquidation of financial assistance to four NGOs/POs amounting to ₱800,000.00.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the City Accountant sent Demand/rem inder letters to various NGOs/POs to liquidate the financial assistance granted to them, copy furnished the COA. The Office of the City Accountant sees to it that the previous financial assistance granted to NGOs/POs has been liquidated before certifying that they have no pending financial assistance.
observations.reason: There were still NGOs/POs not liquidated the financial assistance.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor closely monitor the liquidation of financial assistance/transfer to NGOs/POs, leagues/confederation s and barangays to ensure the settlement within 60 days upon completion of the project or activity or within 30 days upon receipt of the grant per MOA to ensure the implementation of the intended purpose and to safeguard the fund from possible loss.
We recommended also that the City Accountant, before the release of financial assistance, certify that the previous cash advance granted to the NGO/PO has been liquidated.
We recommended further that the City Accountant make correction on the erroneous entries made on the liquidation of financial assistance to four NGOs/POs amounting to ₱800,000.00.
observations.managementAction: Hon Noli Villarosa sent a letter received by COA on October 7, 2019 requesting extension of time to submit other requirements for their liquidation. The League of Sanggunian Secretaries of Occidental Negros (LESSON) Inc. has fully liquidated the financial assistance of ₱100,000.00 on January 31, 2017. The Office of the City Accountant sees to it that the previous financial assistance granted to NGOs/POs has been liquidated before certifying that they have no pending financial assistance.
observations.reason: Awaiting submission by Hon. Villarosa of other required documents for the liquidation of the financial assistance.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor strongly demand from the recipient leagues and federations for the immediate liquidation of the financial assistance given or require the return or refund of the unexpended or unutilized amount pursuant to 3.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012, otherwise, appropriate legal action be instituted.
We recommended that the City Mayor designate a focal person to conduct regular monitoring on project implementation to ascertain that funds granted as financial assistance to league/federation is utilized for the intended purpose.
We recommended that the City Mayor approve the grant of financial assistance to leagues/federations if duly supported with complete required documents and certification by the City Accountant that fund previously transferred has been liquidated and accounted.
observations.managementAction: Hon. Noli Villarosa sent a letter received by COA on October 7, 2019 requesting extension of time to submit other requirements for their liquidation. The City Accountant sent Demand/rem inder letters to various NGOs/POs and leagues to liquidate the financial assistance given to them.
observations.reason: Awaiting submission by Hon. Villarosa of other required documents for the liquidation of the financial assistance.

observations.managementAction: The City Accountant’s Office records the liquidation of financial assistance of barangays based only on the certified photocop ies submitted . Monitoring on the compliance by barangays with the MOA is being undertaken by the City Accounting Office. Monitoring is being conducted and demand/rem inder letters were sent to barangays with unliquidate d financial assistance.
observations.reason: Delay in the submission of the liquidation report and supporting documents by some barangays.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LGU thru the City Social Services and Development focal person, conduct regular monitoring and prepare report on the existence and status of income-generating or livelihood projects of beneficiaries under the SLP of Asosasyon Sang Magagmay Nga Mangingisda and Bacolod Pantawid Livelihood Assistance to determine whether the objective on the grant of seed capital/loan assistance for self-sufficiency was attained; and
The CSSDO demand from the beneficiaries the loan repayment of seed capital to ensure rollback to other members and groups and to attain the intended purpose of SLP.
observations.managementAction: The LGU thru the City Social Services and Development focal person conducts the monitoring and the CSSDO demands from the beneficiaries the loan repayment of seed capital to ensure rollback to other members and groups.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Treasurer immediately send collection or demand letters to 820 delinquent stallholders at Libertad South Public Market, Burgos North Public Market and Central Market.
In case of failure of concerned stallholders to pay their long overdue market rental fees despite the demand, it is recommended that the City Administrator impose the provision of Section 46 of the Revised Market Code of Bacolod City by revoking or cancelling his lease right and ejectment, after observance of due process.
The City Administrator also revoke, after observance of due process, the lease contract of stall owners who sub-leased their stalls or have their privileges as market stall awardees sold to other persons contrary to Sections 19 and 46 of the Revised Market Code of Bacolod City.
The City Treasurer prepare Monthly Report on Due and Demandable Market Rental Receivable to be submitted to the City Administrator Office for proper monitoring and aid in reconciliation of accounts, if needed.
observations.managementAction:
The Office of the City Treasurer sent reply to the Office of the City Administrator on September 25, 2017 that demand letters were sent to Manokan Country Awardees. Summary of Rental Deliquency – Manokan Country as of September 20, 2017 showed a collection of ₱527,742.16 of 7.61% of the total unpaid rental of ₱6,932,284.99. The City Administrator is presently undertaking and continues the process of executing the provision of Sec. 32 – Revocation of Lease.
The City Administrator ensured coordination of Departments for repair and maintenance of markets and that the monthly report on the collection be prepared.
observations.reason: Report from City Treasurer’ s Office on the collection of delinquent stallholde rs was not prepared and submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant prepare Journal Entry Voucher to take up all valid book reconciling items at once after the bank reconciliation process is finished pursuant to Section 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996 in order to ascertain the correctness of balances of Cash in Bank – LCCA. Further, we recommended that the City Accountant immediately make written representation to the bank regarding the various long outstanding bank reconciling items not acted. Otherwise, appropriate adjustments must be prepared for bank discrepancies that the bank will definitely not consider. Henceforth, the City Accountant promptly inform the bank of any error or discrepancy noted in the statement while the documents are still available to ensure that correction is effected in the subsequent bank statement and within the time frame to submit the data needed for reconciliation. The discrepancies should be thoroughly investigated to determine the person(s) responsible for the error, either the city or the bank so it could be promptly instituted.
observations.managementAction:
Reply dated April 4, 2018 sent to COA on even date. The Office of the City Accountant has passed journal entries on various dates to adjust/correct certain reconcilin g items.
The Office of the City Accountant is coordinating with the bank for the prior years’ records for immediate taking up. Gathering of data on several adjustments is still on-going.
observations.reason: Other unreconciled items will be adjusted once supporting documents are available. Some adjustments could not be reflected due to the funds from the national government which were added to the bank account but automatic deposit were not recorded in the books due to the difficulty in the verificati on with the banks since the balances were mostly prior years.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer and the City Accountant reconcile their records per cashbook, general and subsidiary ledgers immediately while documents are still available for references. Henceforth, the two offices must conduct reconciliation at least every end of each quarter and any items subject for correction must be adjusted promptly to present a reliable Cash – Local Treasury balance or the accountable officer’s balance of accountability at any given time as required under Sec. 181 (c), GAAM, Volume I. Moreover, the City Treasurer require the Cashier to rule, foot and certify the cashbook pursuant to Section 26, Volume II of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs.

The City Accountant maintain subsidiary ledger for other revenue collection and disbursing officers for reconciliation purposes with the cashbooks to fully ascertain the correctness of their accountabilities and to safeguard fund from possible loss.

We recommended that the City GAD focal person prepare the Annual GAD Plan and Budget using the prescribed format under Joint Circular No. 2013-01 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA, submit it to the Provincial Planning and Development Office then to the DILG Regional Office for review and approval. Prepare and submit the annual GAD accomplishment report within five (5) working days from the end of January of the preceding year. The report should contain actual accomplishments based on PPAs in GAD Plan with corresponding actual amount paid thereon.

We recommended that the City Mayor cause the allocation in the Annual Budget at least 1% of IRA for LCPC related activities pursuant to Section 15 of Republic Act No. 9344. Further, the City direct the focal person of LCPC to prepare LDPC and LIPC and maximize the LCPC Fund to identified PPAs provided in DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 to ensure full implementation on the protection of children.

We recommended that the City Mayor cause the remittance of premiums, contributions and loan repayments due to GSIS after reconciliation and determination by the City Accountant of the correct amount to avoid further penalties and to ensure the availment of benefits by the officers and personnel members.
We recommended also that the City Accountant secure data supporting the Schedule of Due to GSIS on all funds to determine the correct amount of unremitted premiums, contributions and loan repayments to GSIS. The personnel in-charge reconcile the accounts with debit balance to arrive at a correct amount of Due to GSIS.
observations.managementAction: Remittance of monthly premium contributions to national government regulating agencies like GSIS, Pag-ibig, BIR and Philhealth is done by the City thru the Office of the City Accountant which handles deductions from salaries of employees on or before the 10th day of the month ensuring month. Such that from the MITCS, a disbursement voucher is prepared at the office and check is issued by the City Treasurer’s Office, is religio usly remitted to the concerned government agencies. In the previous years when manual recording was used there maybe inadvertent ce in recordings which accumulate d. In 2007 when eNGAS was instituted , the balance were forwarded as beginning balance in the books in 2007. It can’t be known whether or not it was due to inadvertence or simple non-remittance. There was never a time however when employee was turned down or not admitted to a hospital when contingency occurred. Balance of Philhealth Payable as of December 31, 2017 still includes amounts remitted in January 2018. Balance of Due to Philhealth still includes amount remitted in January 2018. The remittance of Philhealth premium contributions is being conducted.
observations.reason: Monthly reconcilia tion of Philhealth premiu m contributi ons is being conducted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City BAC Secretariat Office submit copies of purchase orders, contracts and supporting documents within five working days from the date of issuance of the P.O./Contracts for timely auditorial review as well as the timely conduct of technical evaluation.
We recommended further that BAC Secretariat ensure that the date of receipt is indicated upon signing for conformity by the supplier on the penalty clause in the PO before submission to the COA Office and for complete documentation.
observations.managementAction:
The BAC Secretariat submitted on February 15, 2018 to the office of the COA their letter-rep ly to this audit observation. The Office of the City Accountant stated that the submission of POs and supporting documents to the Auditor is the primary responsibility of the BAC Secretariat. Therefore, whether or not submitted by BAC Secretariat, the Office of the City Accountant still process any disbursement voucher with review of supporting documents for approval and issuance of check.
The BAC Secretariat stated that the delay on the submission was due to circumstances beyond their control like interrupted wifi connections and access to PhilGEPS website which unable them to post Notice of Awards on time and among other causes.
observations.reason: Per BAC, there was no turn-over of documents by the former BAC when the new BAC Secretariat assumed position.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:
a) The amount of ₱38,885,743.83 was taken up as Other Payables (439-for Trust Fund). However, creation of the account 438-Trust Liabilitie s-DRRM would imperil the other accounts. Thus, in the succeeding years related transactio n however, would still be recorded in the Account 439. The unexpended balance recorded in Account 439 would be transferred to Account 438 every end of the year when financial reports are prepared.
b) ₱277,416.00 were charged to IT Equipment and Software account instead of Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment account. The Work Financial Plan is being prepared by the DRRM personnel and the Obligation Request bearing IT Equipment account emanates from City Budget Office, so that the Office of the City Accountant is not in the position to change the account code of the OBR.
We recommended further that the City Accountant reclassify the procured IT Equipment items charged to IT Equipment and Software account amounting to ₱277,416.00 to Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment (1-07-05-090) account to reflect correct account balances.
observations.reason:
This is due to non-buildi ng up of the Account 438-Trust Liabilitie s-DRRM when the eNGAS Program was introduced by COA in the previous years which did not include the Account Code 438.
The Work Financial Plan is being prepared by the DRRM personnel and the Obligation Request bearing IT Equipment account emanates from City Budget Office, so that the Office of the City Accountant is not in the position to change the account code of the OBR.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Planning and Development Office, the City Engineer’s Office and other offices that are involved in the implementation of 20% Development Fund projects, immediately address the causes of delays and fast track its implementation. Regular monitoring of project implementation should be assigned to specific office to achieve desirable outcomes from the delivery of socio-economic development and environmental services to the constituents.
We recommended further that management should be more aggressive in the implementation of projects to attain higher performance efficiency level.
observations.reason: There were 133 projects not implemente d under the 20% Developmen t fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that City of Bacolod and the Local School Board refrain from charging expenses which are not among the primary purpose of the SEF and strictly adhere to the regulations on the prioritization of expenses chargeable against SEF as provided under Section 272 of the Local Government Code (RA 7160) and Item No. 4.0 of Joint Circular No. 01 s. 2017 of DepEd, DBM and DILG, dated January 19, 2017.
observations.reason: Penalty is still included in the payment to BACIWA.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the monitoring officers issue Daily Monitoring Certificate to ascertain the adequacy/sufficiency of the garbage collection, hauling and disposal services of the contractor and for appropriate action of the DPS; and
The City Mayor thru the Solid Waste Management Board and DPS conduct ocular inspection on the markings and covering materials on all collection trucks to determine actual condition and require the contractor to replace old materials to ensure efficient delivery of garbage collection, hauling and disposal services. Review on the actual performance against the obligations of the contractor provided in the Contract shall be regularly made by the Board and DPS to determine compliance and to impose penalty in case of violations and for corrective action.

We recommended that the incumbent City Mayor demand immediate settlement/liquidation of unliquidated cash advances for Intelligence Operations and for APEC, both were chargeable against Confidential or Intelligence Fund pursuant to General Guidelines of COA Circular No. 2003-003 dated July 30, 2003 and Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015.
observations.managementAction: Except for those who have retired, separated from service, or deceased, the OCA is sending a letter reminder to the employee concerned to settle their unliquidated advances or face sanction based on COA rules. A letter dated March 1, 2019 to COA Chairman Michael G. Aguinaldo was sent on March 4, 2019 for follow-up on the receipt of Liquidation Report of cash advance of Pacita Tero, SDO.
observations.reason: Sanction like withholding of salary to active employees with unliquidat ed cash advances was not yet implemente d.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant ensure that all the basic requirements for procurement through public bidding are complete before certifying the disbursement vouchers pursuant to Items 9.0, 9.1 and 9.1.3.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012; Additionally, adequate controls should be instituted in the disbursement of government funds to attain the objectives of the program and projects of the City, safeguard its resources and promote efficiency and economy in the use thereof; The City Mayor approve only the payment of procured items through public bidding if duly supported with complete and appropriate supporting documents; and The BAC Chairman or the BAC Secretariat submit to COA Office the PO and contract together with supporting documents within five days after its perfection pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001.
observations.reason: There were still disburse ments with incomplete documentat ion pertaining to procurement of equipment, constructi on materials and infrastru cture projects thru public bidding.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer immediately send collection or demand letters to 23 delinquent stallholders at Manokan Country. In case of failure of concerned stallholders to pay their long overdue market rental fees despite the demand, it is recommended that the City Administrator impose the provision of Section 46 of the Revised Market Code of Bacolod City by revoking or cancelling their lease right and ejectment, after observance of due process. The City Mayor make sure that contract of lease shall be strictly renewed every two years from date the contract is signed pursuant to the provision of the Contract of Lease. The City Legal Office take action on those illegal vendors selling on the streets outside the public markets to give way to legitimate market vendors who pays rentals to the City. The City Administrator ensures the coordination of all Departments, i.e., City Treasurer’s Office, City Administrator’s Office, City Legal Office and City Engineers Office who have roles and responsibilities in the full implementation of the Market Code and other related City Ordinances.
observations.managementAction:
The Office of the City Treasurer sent reply to the Office of the City Administrator on September 25, 2017 that demand letters were sent to Manokan Country Awardees. Summary of Rental Deliquency – Manokan Country as of September 20, 2017 showed a collection of ₱527,742.16 of 7.61% of the total unpaid rental of ₱6,932,284.99. The City Administrator is presently undertaking and continues the process of executing the provision of Sec. 32 – Revocation of Lease.
Presently, one market inspector was assigned to inspect cash ticket or official receipts issued to awardees and transient vendors. There is an existing Local Market Committee who is set to meet when there are urgent matters needed to be taken up. The City Administrator initiates the revocation and cancellation of delinquent lease contracts thru due process to be able to offer these blocks, stalls and tables to interested vendors who are willing to pay and to stop the increasing uncollected market rental dues. The City Administrator ensures the coordination of Departments for repair and maintenance of markets and that the monthly report on the collection be prepared.
observations.reason: Without update report on the collection from delinquent stallholde rs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer follow-up with the SP the issuance of resolution for the return/transfer of dormant funds to the National Agencies which granted the fund or revert back the dormant accounts to the National Treasury or Current Account of the General Fund of the City based on the MOA.
observations.managementAction: The City has started paying the loans and partially utilized the loan proceeds.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the DRRM Officer facilitate the immediate installation of the Automatic Transfer Switch and the distribution of Emergency Back Pack Kits to the identified rescuers and responders so the purpose of the purchase will be realized and also to prevent wastage of government resources.
We recommended that the LDRRM Council prepare the annual LDRRM Investment Plan pursuant to 5.1.2 And Annex A of COA Circular No. 2012-002 by specifying therein the projects, programs and activities to be implemented under the Mitigation Fund. The Annual Work and Financial Plan of the DRRM Fund prepared by the DRRM Office should only serve as a supporting document to the LDRRMFIP considering that it is not required under the said COA Circular.
observations.managementAction: The City has started implementing the new system and started using the new form for Receipt of Collection and Deposit prescribed by the BLGF in the later part of 2014. The MITCS Office started to implement the new system and started using the new form for Receipt of Collection and Deposit prescribed by the BLGF in the later part of 2014.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LGU thru the CSSDO monitor regularly the existence of income-generating or livelihood projects to determine whether the objective of the loan assistance for income augmentation on the beneficiaries was attained. The CSSDO officer in-charge demand from the beneficiaries the full and timely payment of loan amortization to ensure the relending to other members and groups and to safeguard the fund from possible loss and mismanagement. The individual beneficiaries remit directly to the City Treasurer’s Office on time and in full the monthly amortization paid by the beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction: The CSSDO monitor regularly the existence of income-generating or livelihood projects and demand the repayment.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor ascertain if the City will continue the transfer or donation of the four units motorcycles to MTCC, Bacolod City. In case the City pursue said donation or assistance to MTCC, Bacolod City, the donation or transfer must be covered by a Deed of Donation duly ratified by the SP of the City to make the transfer valid as provided in Section 186 of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 23, 1992.
The Heads of Offices of the City ascertain the requirement needs of their Office when preparing any request for approval of the City Mayor to avoid wastage of government resources.
Equipment or other government property shall be issued only in accordance with the purpose and for the intended users they were procured.

We recommended that the City Mayor thru the Solid Waste Management Board facilitate the speedy siting and establishment of a sanitary landfill to conform to the requirements of Sections 40 and 41 of RA 9003 in order to achieve an effective solid waste disposal system for the City.
The City Officials involved in solid waste management to comply with the requirements of said Act to avoid administrative sanction.
observations.reason: Sanitary land fill was not yet established.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor or the Head of the Procuring Entity (HOPE) determine whether the BAC and TWG members have been remiss in the performance of their functions in the procurement of the learning materials. Further, he must require the BAC and TWG members to submit their valid justifications for not able to ascertain the deficient eligibility documents submitted by D and L Printing and Publishing House.
The City Mayor strengthen the procurement function of the present BAC and TWG members or may designate new BAC and TWG members if warranted to increase operational efficiency and effectiveness in the City’s procurement process to ensure that only eligible and qualified prospective bidders are allowed to participate in the bidding of the various procurement needs of the City.
The BAC Chairman must recommend to the Head of Procuring Entity (HOPE) or the City Mayor to impose an administrative penalty of suspension to D & L Printing and Publishing House for submitting eligibility requirements containing false information or falsified documents and enforce the forfeiture of bid security or the performance security posted by the concerned bidder pursuant to Sec. 69.1 and 69.2 of R.A. 9184.
observations.managementAction: A new set of BAC and TWG members were appointed by the City Mayor. A new set of evaluation is made to assess the operational efficiency and effectiveness of the procurement process.
observations.reason: Some BAC members started paying the disallowan ce through payroll deduction. However, the City was not able to collect the disallowance from former BAC members and retired members.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor designate Appraisal Committee to assess the current value of the three gymnasiums in order to present a reliable value of the properties in the books.
The OIC, City Accountant prepare adjustment on the cost of the three gymnasiums based on the appraisal report of the Committee by debiting the appraised value of the three gymnasiums and a corresponding deduction on unidentified PPE-building account under the SEF books.
The OIC, City Accountant immediately make adjustment by adding the cost of Improvement of the three gymnasiums and Construction of one Covered Court including purchase of Geographical Information System as addition to PPE accounts to reflect reliable balance of Property, Plant and Equipment accounts. In addition, it is also essential to prepare adjustment for all maintenance and operating expenses charged against the DRRMF to reflect the correct amount of DRRM expenses for the year 2014.
The City Accountant adhere to the guidelines to transfer all unexpended/unobligat ed balance of the Quick Response Fund and the DRRMF-MOOE for the current year including CYs 2013 and 2012 DRRMF balances to the Special Trust Fund under the Account “Trust Liability-DRRM” in the Trust Fund Books pursuant to 5.1.10 COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the OIC, City Accountant immediately remind the DepEd, Bacolod City to submit a report of disbursements certified by the DepEd Accountant, approved by the Superintendent and duly audited by the Audit Team Leader and submit a copy of Credit Notice or copy of Official Receipt issued for the refund of unexpended balance of fund transferred pursuant to Item 3.1.3 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.
Should DepEd, Bacolod City opted to comply with the Affidavit of Undertaking, then the City Accountant require that Office to submit the pertinent documents for the liquidation of the amount transferred/granted as financial assistance. Likewise, the City Accountant ensure that no succeeding release of financial assistance shall be allowed unless the previous one was fully liquidated or accounted for and require that each fund transferred to national agency like DepEd be supported with a Memorandum of Agreement pursuant to Item 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant immediately sent a letter to the Bacolod City Superinten dent as a way of reminding to submit the liquidation reports.
observations.reason: Liquidation was not fully undertaken.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to prepare as soon as possible the necessary adjustment to book up the reversion of the remaining unutilized PDAF funds to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund and the City Treasurer to draw the disbursement voucher covering the return of the unutilized PDAF funds to the National Treasury.
observations.managementAction: The City prepared checks in the total amount of ₱1,934,322.00 for the return of the unutilized PDAF funds.
observations.reason: Reversion was not fully undertaken. However, the amount of ₱1,834,322.00 totaling six checks have become stale.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City’s GAD focal person ensure that programs and projects are implemented in accordance with approved GAD Plan and Budget of the City to address the gender issues and concerns specified therein.
observations.managementAction: The City prepared checks in the total amount of ₱1,934,322.00 for the return of the unutilized PDAF funds.
observations.reason: Reversion was not fully undertaken. The amount of ₱1,834,322.00 totaling six checks have become stale.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the payment of job hires be supported with specific job description to ensure that expenditures are valid charges against the LDRRM fund. The OIC, City Accountant ensure that program, project and activities to be charged against the LDRRM Fund conform with the guidelines in 5.0 of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 26, 2013.
Notices of Suspension totaling ₱897,164.00 be settled through compliance with the requirements indicated therein.
observations.reason: Notices of Suspension totaling ₱897,164.00 be settled through compliance with the requirements indicated therein.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: The Department of Public Services, City Environment and Natural Resources Office and the General Services Office as well as the Bacolod City Solid Waste Management Board consider the following: Develop strategies to check the major activities of the contractor, define clearly the activities to be monitored to ensure delivery of satisfactory service and provide parameters to assess the efficiency of the contractor and require them to prepare a monitoring report at the end of the day; Assign Monitoring Team/monitors in specific areas that they could possibly cover within a day to properly conduct monitoring activities and establish a representative percentage for each activity to be monitored to arrive at a valid conclusion on the performance of the contractor and require them to prepare a monitoring report; Document violations committed by the contractor and submit a report to the immediate Supervisor; Require the Monitoring Team to submit semi monthly report to the Solid Waste Management Board and other offices concerned as supporting document of the contractor’s claim and guidance or reference in evaluating the contractor’s performance; Enhance feedback mechanism wherein constituents could report the complaints on the failure to collect waste or unsatisfactory performance of the contractor through hotlines or regular surveys.
observations.managementAction: A new set of BAC and TWG members were appointed by the City Mayor. A new set of evaluation is made to assess the operational efficiency and effectiveness of the procurement process.
observations.reason: Monitoring is undertaken by the DPS but there were terms and conditions in MOA not complied with to fully assess the efficiency of collection and hauling accomplishments of the contractor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor efficiently utilize the loan proceeds for purpose for which the loan was secured to ensure the effective delivery of planned program to target beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction: The City has started paying the loans and partially utilized the loan proceeds.
observations.reason: Loans are partially paid.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: The City Treasurer strictly require collecting officers to turnover/remit their collections timely and intact particularly at year end to avoid accumulation of cash in vault to huge amount as at financial statement date and to further avert funds from possible misappropriation, misapplication or loss for any illegitimate reason pursuant to Section 29 on the Manual on NGAS for LGUs Volume I. The Liquidating Officer verify collections turned over by the collectors per Report of Collection and Deposit as against the Official Receipts issued and check the Statement of Accountable Forms to determine the movement of accountable forms in the possession of the collectors including inventory of forms on hand. The MITCS Office prepare a report on the result of its verification of the Official Receipts as reported by the collectors in the RCD especially in case of adverse or unfavorable observations so management can install necessary controls to address the risks.
observations.managementAction: The MITCS Office started to implement the new system and started using the new form for Receipt of Collection and Deposit prescribed by the BLGF in the later part of 2014.
observations.reason: New forms of RCD issued by BLGF were available towards the end of 2014.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant require the Chairs and members of the Bids and Awards and Technical Working Group to refund the excess honoraria received. We further recommended that the City Accountant ensure that payment of honoraria to the Chairs and Members of the BAC and TWG is in accordance with the guidelines prescribed in Budget Circular No. 2004-5A dated October 7, 2005.
observations.managementAction: The OCA is currently paying the honoraria of the BAC and its technical working group based on their monthly collection for ITBs and bid document fees. The excess payment to the BAC & Technical working group is being deducted from their honoraria.
observations.reason: Some BAC members started paying the disallowanc e through payroll deduction. However, the City was not able to collect the disallowance from former BAC members and retired members.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Government GAD focal person prepare the GAD Accomplishment Report following the form prescribed in Annex B of Item 5.2 of Joint Circular No 2004-01 of DBM, NEDA and NCRFW (now Philippine Commission on Women). Prepare and submit the consolidated Annual GAD Accomplishment Report for CY 2013 of concerned offices prepared in accordance with what is prescribed under Joint Circular No. 2004-01 of DBM, NEDA and NCRFW. The report should contain actual accomplishments vis-à-vis targets as well the amounts utilized for the achievement of such.
observations.managementAction: The program and the plan were prepared and were submitted to the GAD focal person.
observations.reason: The GAD focal person was only designated on the last quarter of 2014. GAD accomplishment reports of offices implementing GAD program, activities and projects were not gathered and consolidated and submitted to the concerned offices on time.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor/Board of Trustees of the Provident Fund submit immediately the authorization and guidelines from the DBM allowing subsequent government monetary contributions to the Provident Fund. The City Accountant ensure that no fund transfers to the Provident Fund will be released without prior authority and guidelines issued by the DBM.
observations.managementAction: The City government for the past years are no longer computing nor paying Provident Fund the supposed counterpa rt government share of the city. The former City Mayor suspended the appropriat ion and disbursem ent of government share of Provident Fund per memorandum dated January 16, 2012 to the City Accountant, Budget Officer and City Treasurer. The City Mayor also instructed that the City Accountant account for the total government share given to the Provident Fund since the time of the creation of the fund in 1995 up to December 31, 2009.
observations.reason: The Board of Trustees of the Provident Fund failed to submit until now the guidelines from the DBM allowing government monetary contributions to the Fund.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and the General Services Officer to determine the causes of discrepancies between the Accounting and GSO records and require them to submit a report on the result of their verification.
On the basis of their report, the City Mayor instruct the two Offices to undertake definite measures to resolve the discrepancy in order to reflect reliable balances of inventories.
We recommended also that the City Accountant to meanwhile, reclassify to Other Assets account and the GSO to dispose the 1,067 unserviceable PPE items amounting to ₱18,763,431.10 to reflect the fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements.
observations.reason: No definite measure was undertaken to determine the causes of the discrepancies until now.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor compel the contractors to pay the liquidated damages amounting to P22,296,847.43. The City Accountant should immediately deduct the liquidated damages from any amounts due to the contractors. The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) should blacklist all contractors concerned who incurred liquidated damages.
The BAC Chairman must recommend to the Head of Procuring Entity (HOPE) or the City Mayor to impose an administrative penalty of suspension to D & L Printing and Publishing House for submitting eligibility requirements containing false information or falsified documents and enforce the forfeiture of bid security or the performance security posted by the concerned bidder pursuant to Sec. 69.1 and 69.2 of R.A. 9184.
observations.managementAction:
Papers forwarded to COA on April 2012. In future all communications between the contractor and the CEO, the COA should be furnished a copy.
The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) should blacklist all contractors concerned who incurred liquidated damages.
observations.reason: Liquidated damages were not actually collected from the suppliers because the City Mayor approved the extension due to variation and change orders.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor thru its City GAD Council must formulate their annual GAD Plan, with specific and tangible programs, activities and projects, within the context of their mandate as well as its budgetary requirements.
The conduct of massive information, education and communication campaigns on the gender issue/s must also be integrated as part of such Plan.
The City GAD Council shall prepare their annual GAD accomplishment report for the previous year that contains actual accomplishments vis-à-vis targets as well the amounts utilized for the achievement of such.
observations.managementAction: To follow recommendation of COA.
observations.reason: The GAD focal person was only designated on the last quarter of 2014.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that – The City Treasurer’s Office should exert effort to collect rental receivable and device an arrangement or scheme to collect the due and demandable rental receivable.
The City Treasurer’s Office should direct Market Collectors to prepare a Monthly Report on Due and Demandable Market Rental Receivable to be submitted to the City Administrator’s Office for proper monitoring and aid in reconciliation of accounts, if needed.
The City Legal Office take action on those illegal vendors selling on the streets outside the public markets to give way to legitimate market vendors who pays rentals to the City.
The City Administrator should assign a Market Inspector that will do its duties and responsibilities to the betterment of market operations.
The City Mayor should create a Market Committee who will formulate programs and activities to improve income of market vendors like: organize promotional activities involving the public markets (including Masskara Festival, Bacolaodiat Festival, Food Trades, etc.) to boost the occupancy rate of public markets, as well as to attract and broaden patronage; allow flexibility to prospective tenants, e.g., stalls left vacant for over 6 months be allowed an Open-market Rental, or lent out stalls to short-term tenancy.
observations.managementAction:
The City Administrator is presently undertaking and continues the process of executing the provision of Sec. 32 – Revocation of Lease. Several demand letters and notices were sent to delinquent awardees. There is an existing Local Market Committee who is set to meet when there are urgent matters needed to be taken up. The City Administrator initiates the revocation and cancellation of delinquent lease contracts through due process to be able to offer these blocks, stalls and tables to interested vendors who are willing to pay and to stop the increasing uncollected market rental dues.
The City Administrator ensures the coordination of Departments for repair and maintenance of markets and that the monthly report on the collection be prepared.
observations.reason: Ongoing activity such as sending first and final demand letters to top delinquent awardees.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor ensure the compliance of all accountable officers with the provisions of COA Circular 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
The City Treasurer and the Heads of Departments or Divisions to closely supervise the accountable officers under their jurisdiction to ensure prompt liquidation of their cash advances.
The Accountable Officers submit a status report of all cash advances granted and liquated by them to the office of City Accountant, especially to those with long outstanding cash advances.
The City Accountant review the status report of accountable officers and make necessary action to correct and adjust their accounts after proper accounting have been made.
The City Accountant monitor the liquidation of cash advances before a new one is granted and exert extra effort to cause the liquidation or settlement of the long outstanding cash advances.
observations.managementAction: Except for those who have retired, separated from service, or deceased, the OCA is sending a letter reminder to the employee concerned to settle their unliquidated advances or face sanction based on COA rules.
observations.reason: Sanction like withholding of salary to active employees with unliquidat ed cash advances was not yet implemente d.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
Justify the P340.00 per square meter purchase price paid for Felisa Solid Waste Disposal Facility;
Justify why the lots were classified as Residential Zone per CPDO certification when the classification in the declaration of the real property taxes was Agricultural; and
Cause the reduction of the purchase price for the acquired property considering that the dumpsite facility cannot be accessed and be useful to the City Government except thru the unappraised roads right of way.
observations.managementAction: Papers forwarded to COA Region 6 on July 12, 2012 as part of Appeal to Notice of Disallowance and recommendation letter C was assailed in the said appeal. Noted is the filing of case by the Landowner to the LGU for the collection of the remaining balance of the purchase price.
observations.reason: Decision of COA Region VI on the Pending Appeal vs. Notice of Disallowan ce not yet released.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor refrain from appropriating further any public funds to be contributed as government share to the Provident Fund. Direct the City Budget Officer and the City Treasurer to refrain from obligating and disbursing any amount already earmarked as government share to the Provident Fund for Calendar Year 2010. Direct the City Accountant to account for the total government share given to the Provident Fund since the time of the creation of the fund in 1995 up to December 31, 2009.
observations.managementAction: The former City Mayor suspended the appropriation and disbursement of government share of Provident Fund per memorandum dated January 16, 2012 to the City Accountant, Budget Officer and City Treasurer. The City’s counsel of former City Mayor has requested to defer submission of comments pending opinion/resolution of DBM Central Office on the matter.
observations.reason: The accounting of total government share paid to the Provident Fund since 1995 up to 2009 was not submitted to COA Office. The Board of Trustees of the Provident Fund failed to submit until now the guidelines from the DBM allowing government monetary contributions to the Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to require the barangays to submit documents to prove that the fund given by the City of Bacolod were actually spent for the purpose they requested and that accounting and auditing rules have been observed in its disbursement; refrain from giving another or additional financial assistance to the barangays who failed to present documents in compliance with their respective undertaking; establish a monitoring procedure and assign personnel to evaluate the effectiveness of the implementation of the Solid Waste Management Program given to the barangays; and justify why the 16 barangays were not extended financial assistance to implement the Solid Waste Management Program of the City.
observations.managementAction: Financial Assistance to some Barangays for solid waste management was partially liquidated as of 2014. Barangay with another financial assistance were not approved of their disbursements pending liquidation/settlement of their previous aid.
observations.reason: Failure of some barangays to submit liquidation report of the amount granted as financial assistance for solid waste management.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
Direct the City Accountant to require the submission of complete documents from the beneficiaries of PAGCOR funds; together with the Sangguniang Panlungsod: review the City’s utilization of PAGCOR financial assistance vis-à-vis the guiding spirit for PAGCOR’s the voluntary assistance; and prioritize projects and livelihood that is in line with PAGCOR’s purpose for the upliftment of the socio-economic conditions of the less-privileged.
observations.managementAction: The COA sent a letter to the CMO and the SP informing of the same, however some councilors are reluctant to submit complete documents from some SP members on whose recommendation the financial assistance was made possible. The COA was furnished copies of OCAs letter to the CMO and the SP.
observations.reason: PAGCOR funds were used mostly as financial assistance to individuals instead of utilizing the same for projects that will address the needs of the less privileged.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: direct the City Legal Officer to file appropriate criminal charges against the persons liable and responsible for the above-discussed deficiencies; direct the CGSO to revisit and review the procedures involved in the property management and strengthen the internal control to address the lapses in the procedures; direct the CGSO to conduct an inventory of all unserviceable motor vehicles that had been returned to the CGSO, protect and prevent the cannibalization of these vehicles and speed up their disposal.
observations.managementAction: I & I report already accomplished and submitted to COA Region VI for disposal.
observations.reason: Awaiting action from COA.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Property Officer: to inspect the above donated properties to determine their existence and whereabouts, their present condition, the provision of protection further deterio ration and cannibaliza tion; prepare, maintain record and submit the documents for these properties like the Property Ledger Cards, ARE, Property Return Slip; request for the proper disposal if found unserviceable; make a report on the irregular and improper use, losses and cannibalization of the said properties and recommend for the filing of charges against all persons liable; and identify properties which can be easily processed and make adjustments in the books of the city.
observations.managementAction: Data on donated properties are not available. No record as to the transfer of property. Identify properties which can be easily processed and make adjustments in the books of the city.
observations.reason: Until now, no action was undertaken by management.

observations.recommendation:
a.) The Local Chief Executive should ensure that the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997, and Section 4(6) and 89 of P.D. 1445 in granting cash advances be complied with.

b.) Require the Municipal Treasurer to submit all the liquidations reports on time pursuant to Section 5 of COA Circular 97-002.

c.) Require the Acting Municipal Accountant to closely monitor the granting and liquidation of cash advances. Send letter of demand to employees of their unliquidated cash advances.
observations.managementAction: The cash advances granted to official and employees was mostly incurred only for the month of December 2017. Since there is a grace period of 30 days, the liquidation report of the said cash advances was done on the following month, because official and employees were not granted another cash advances if no liquidation were being made for the previous cash advances. The Municipal Treasurer will submit reports on time.
observations.reason: Demand Letters Prepared and Distributed to Concerned Employees.

observations.recommendation:
a.) The Local Chief Executive should refrain from granting of cash advances to Elective Officials other than their traveling expenses.

b.) Require the Acting Municipal Accountant to closely monitor the granting and liquidation of cash advances. Send letter of demand to employees of their unliquidated cash advances.
observations.managementAction: Granting of Cash Advances to Elective Officials shall be refrained other than traveling expenses. The Municipal Accountant will exercise control and supervision in the prepared itinerary of travel of the concerned Municipality’s officers and employees and requiring them to refund excess amount of the travelling expenses claimed to concerned officials and employees.
observations.reason: Demand Letters Prepared and Distributed to Concerned Employees.

observations.recommendation:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to maintain Subsidiary Ledgers for each Revenue Collectors.

Require the Revenue Collectors to submit their Report of Collections and Deposits to the accounting division individually.
observations.managementAction:
Subsidiary ledgers for Revenue Collectors now maintained and updated with Revenue Collector’s Report.

Report of Collections and Deposits to the accounting division individually submitted to MACCO.

observations.recommendation:
a.) The Local Chief Executive should create an Inventory Committee to conduct complete physical inventory count of all PPE and submit Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE). 

b.) Instruct the General Services Officer/Supply Officer and the Municipal Accountant to regularly reconcile their records and review the composition of the PPE account to come up with an accurate Property, Plant and Equipment account balance.
observations.managementAction: The General Services Office and the Accounting Office are now maintaining Stock and Property Cards and Supplies, and Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger cards based on the result of inventory. The Local Chief issued Executive Order creating the Inventory Committee with a mandate to update and conduct inventory count of all PPE and submit Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) led by the General Services Officer and Supply Officer. The Municipal Accountant and Supply Officer has regularly reconciled their records and review the composition of the PPE account to come up with an accurate Property, Plant and Equipment account balance.
observations.reason: (Reiterated in this 2018 AAR)

observations.recommendation: The General Services Officer and the Municipal Accountant should maintain Stock and Property Cards and Supplies and Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards, respectively, pursuant to paragraphs 3 and 4, Section 114, Chapter 7, Volume 1 of the National Government Accounting Manual for Local Government Units. The required records shall also be the basis by the General Services Office for its reconciliation with the actual physical inventory.
observations.managementAction: The General Services Office and the Accounting Office are now maintaining Stock and Property Cards and Supplies, and Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards based on the result of the inventory. The Local Chief issued Executive Order creating the Inventory Committee with a mandate to update and conduct inventory count of all PPE and submit Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) led by the Supply Officer regularly reconcile their records and review the composition of the PPE account to come up with an accurate Property, Plant and Equipment account balance and reconciliation with actual physical inventory.
observations.reason: Management has already maintained the Stocks and Property Cards, and Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards.

observations.recommendation:
a.) 	Direct the Acting Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere to the prescribed time frame for the submission of monthly and quarterly reports as well as transmitting all receipts and disbursement records with complete supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor.

b.)	Direct the Acting Municipal Accountant to closely coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer for the latter to submit immediately the required reports within the prescribed period, and to facilitate the recording of the transactions in the books of accounts.

c.) 	Direct the Municipal Treasurer to submit on time and forward to the Accounting Office the financial reports to facilitate the recording of the transactions in the books of accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accounting Office help in finalizing the reports, conducted review and facilitated submission of monthly and quarterly reports as well as transmitting all receipts and disbursement records with complete supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.reason: Management exerted effort to comply the submission of mandatory reports within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation:
a.) 	Direct the Municipality’s officers and employees to impose proper application of the entitlement of traveling allowance as well as completeness and regularity of the essential documents like travel orders, bus tickets, e-ticket, boarding pass and itinerary of travel. 

b.) 	Require the employees concerned to refund the excess amount of the travelling expenses claimed.
observations.managementAction: The Local Chief Executive directed the officers and employees to impose proper application of the entitlement of travelling allowance as well as completeness and regularity of essential supporting documents. The Municipal Accountant will exercise control and supervision in the prepared itinerary of travel of the concerned Municipality’s officers and employees and requiring them to refund excess amount of the travelling expenses claimed to concerned officials and employees.

observations.recommendation: Direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Secretariat to comply with the regulations and furnish the audit team with the required copy of contracts along with their supporting documents within the prescribed period for the timely review, evaluation and inspection of the same.
observations.managementAction: The BAC will comply to the submission of technical documents as required by your office based on the prescribed Bidding procedure and protocol based RA 9184. The BAC Secretariat shall comply with the regulations and furnish the audit team with the required copy of contracts along with their supporting documents within the prescribed period for timely review, evaluation and inspection. We will submit to your office copies of the contracts before issuance of award and notice to proceed. The Municipal Engineer, Supply Officer and the End User of the Procurement with the Accounting Office shall facilitate the request and conduct of technical inspection for all implemented projects/
observations.reason: (Reiterated in this 2018 AAR)

observations.recommendation: Require the GAD Focal Person to prepare and submit a copy of the DILG approved annual GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) and the corresponding GAD Accomplishment Report within the period prescribed under COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014.
observations.managementAction: The agency submitted a copy of its DILG approved 2018 Plan and Budgets but failed to submit its accomplishment report.
observations.reason: (Reiterated in the 2018 AAR)

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation:

          We recommended that the GSO update  records  on  acquisitions  and
    disposals in the PPE cards for Machinery and Equipment,  Furniture  and
    Fixtures,  Transportation  Equipment   and   other   PPEs  for  reconciliation
    with APIR, AREs and with subsidiary  ledgers  maintained  at  the  City
    Accountant’s Office. Regular reconciliation shall be conducted  by  the
    GSO  to  ascertain  the  correctness  of  the  PPE  accounts  balances
    and  for  financial  statements  presentation.


          We recommended further that the GSO  maintain  property  cards  on
    Land,  Land  Improvements,   Buildings   and   Other   Structures   for
    reconciliation with balances shown in the Subsidiary Ledgers maintained
    at the City Accountant’s Office.


          We recommended that the Physical Inventory Committee  submit  list
    of PPEs not found during the conduct of  physical  count  to  determine
    losses and liability of persons accountable as provided in Section  149
    of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 23, 1992.


          We recommended that the City Accountant to  meanwhile,  reclassify
    to Other Assets account and the GSO to dispose the 1,067  unserviceable
    PPE items amounting to ₱18,763,431.10 to reflect the fair  presentation
    of accounts in the financial statements.

Recommendation:

          We recommended that the City Engineer’s Office  regularly  prepare
    and submit Status Report on the Accomplishment of Projects as basis  by
    the City Accountant’s Office in transferring/reclassifying CIP accounts
    to appropriate PPE and Public  Infrastructure  accounts  for  completed
    projects.


          We further recommended that  the  City  Accountant  and  the  City
    Engineer’s Office exert efforts in the reconciliation of their  records
    regarding completed and on-going projects.


          We recommended also that  the  City  Mayor  and  the  Acting  City
    Engineer immediately address the problems encountered on  unimplemented
    projects  and  speed  up  the  implementation  of  both  partially  and
    unimplemented projects to avoid losses and to attain the intended
    purposes.

Recommendation:

          We recommended that:

          a)  The  City  Mayor   prioritize   the   demand   for   immediate
             settlement/liquidation of unliquidated cash  advances  pursuant
             to Item 6.0 of COA Circular  No.  2012-004  for  those  granted
             before December 31, 2011;


          b) The City Mayor and the City Accountant strongly ensure that  no
             additional cash advance shall be allowed  to  any  official  or
             employee unless the previous one given to him is first  settled
             or a proper accounting thereof is made pursuant to Section  89,
             PD No. 1445;


          c) The City Mayor require the City Accountant or assign  personnel
             in-charge to follow-up the demand letters sent to officials and
             employees who failed to submit  liquidation  reports  of  their
             cash advance after the prescribed period, otherwise,  implement
             the withholding of the payment of salary especially  to  active
             officials and employees with outstanding cash advances;
          d) The City Accountant likewise send follow-up letter to personnel
             assigned    in    other    government    agencies     demanding
             liquidation/settlement/refund  of   their   unliquidated   cash
             advances.  Make further representation on this  regard  to  the
             Head of the Agency concerned and its Accountant;


          e) The City Mayor  file  a  request  for  authority  to  write-off
             dormant unliquidated cash advances as provided in  Section  8.2
             of COA Circular No. 2016-005, dated December 19, 2016, which is
             the “Guidelines and Procedures  on  the  Write-off  of  Dormant
             Receivable  Accounts,  Unliquidated  Cash  Advances,  and  Fund
             Transfers  of  National  Government  Agencies   (NGAs),   Local
             Government Units (LGUs)  and  Government-Owned  and  Controlled
             Corporations (GOCCs); and


          f) The City Accountant conduct regular and periodic  verification,
             analysis, and validation of the existence of  the  unliquidated
             cash advances,  prepare the necessary adjusting entries for any
             correction,   reclassification   and   recovery/settlement   of
             previously written off accounts, and prepare aging  of  dormant
             unliquidated cash advances to support the request for write-off
             as provided in Section 7 of COA Circular No. 2016-005.

Recommendation:

          We recommended that the City Mayor, through the City Legal  Office
    and City Accounting Office, require the NGOs/POs to submit  liquidation
    report for financial assistance whose programs, projects or  activities
    were  already  completed.  Moreover,  the  City  Mayor  should  designate
    a focal person/office to  take  charge  of  the  regular  monitoring  and
    inspection on the accomplishment of the intended purpose.


          We recommended also that the City Accountant, before  the  release
    of financial assistance, certify that the previous cash advance granted
    to the NGO/PO has been  liquidated,  liquidation  documents  are  post-
    audited and properly taken up in the books to ascertain the validity of
    liquidation.


          We recommended that the City Accountant  make  correction  on  the
    erroneous entries made on the liquidation of  financial  assistance  to
    four NGOs/POs amounting to ₱800,000.00.

Recommendation:

          We recommended that the City Mayor closely monitor the liquidation
    of financial assistance/transfer  to  NGOS/POs,  leagues/confederations
    and barangays to ensure the settlement within 60 days  upon  completion
    of the project or activity or within 30 days upon receipt of the  grant
    per MOA to ensure the implementation of the  intended  purpose  and  to
    safeguard the fund from possible loss.

Recommendation:

          We recommended that  the  City  Mayor  strongly  demand  from  the
    recipient leagues and federations for the immediate liquidation of  the
    financial assistance given or require  the  return  or  refund  of  the
    unexpended or unutilized amount pursuant to 3.1.2 of COA  Circular  No.
    2012-001 dated June 14, 2012, otherwise, appropriate  legal  action  be
    instituted.


          We recommended also that the City  Mayor  designate  personnel  to
    conduct constant monitoring of project implementation to ascertain that
    funds granted as financial assistance to league/federation is  utilized
    for the intended purpose.


          We recommended that the City Mayor approve and the City Accountant
    certify as to the completeness of supporting documents on  the  release
    of fund transfer to  league,  if  aside  from  other  requirements,  is
    supported with MOA and Certification by the Accountant.




          We further recommended to stop granting the  financial  assistance
    unless the previous one is first liquidated.

Recommendation:

          We recommended that:


          a) The City Accountant strongly demand the  immediate  liquidation
             of  fund  transfer/financial  assistance  granted  to   various
             barangays in CYs 2015 to 2017 by setting specific timelines for
             the liquidation considering that the terms of the agreement had
             already been violated;


          b) The City Mayor approve  the  release  of  any  subsequent  fund
             transfer and the City Accountant certify on the completeness of
             supporting documents only if supported  with  Certification  by
             Accountant that previous fund transfer has been liquidated  and
             accounted for in the books;


          c) The City  Accountant  must  exert  extra  efforts  in  securing
             documents  especially  pertaining  to  prior  years’   financial
             assistance  amounting  to  ₱1,315,000.00  and  ascertain  if  a
             corresponding liability with the City is recorded in  the  said
             barangay  so  that  the  City  could  require   the   immediate
             liquidation.  Otherwise,  authority  for  write-off  shall  be
             requested by the City Mayor in accordance with  the  guidelines
             provided in COA Circular No. 2016-005.  Further,  prior  years’
             subsidies from PAGCOR, RPTA, SEF  and  Trust  Funds  and  those
             without details should  also  be  written-off  to  arrive  with
             correct and valid Due from LGUs account;


          d) The City Mayor immediately assign focal person  responsible  in
             regular monitoring on the compliance  by  barangays  and  other
             LGUs  with  the  terms  and  conditions  in  the  MOA  as  well
             as validation of  actual  accomplishments  vis-à-vis  purpose
             for which the fund transfer was granted; and


          e) The City Accountant reclassify the overliquidation of financial
             assistance amounting to ₱2,040.00 to Due to LGUs account (418).

Recommendations:

          We recommended that:


          a) The LGU thru the City Social  Services  and  Development  focal
             person, conduct regular monitoring and prepare  report  on  the
